title: Image Generation Platform — System Design
description: Design a text-to-image generation service: take a prompt, run an expensive GPU diffusion model, and return a safe image. The themes are async GPU job processing on scarce hardware, priority/fair scheduling and batching, input/output safety, and result delivery.

nodes.label: Client
nodes.description: Submits a text prompt and polls/streams for the generated image.

nodes.label: Generation API
nodes.description: Accepts prompts, enforces quota, runs input safety, enqueues jobs.

nodes.label: Job Store
nodes.description: Authoritative job records, status, and result references.

nodes.label: Input Safety Filter
nodes.description: Blocks disallowed prompts before any GPU work.

nodes.label: Priority Job Queues
nodes.description: Per-tier (free/paid/realtime) queues feeding the GPU workers.

nodes.label: GPU Scheduler
nodes.description: Assigns jobs to GPU workers; batches and honors priority.

nodes.label: GPU Inference Workers
nodes.description: Run the diffusion model on a GPU to produce images.

nodes.label: Model / Weights Store
nodes.description: Versioned model weights loaded by workers.

nodes.label: Output Safety Filter
nodes.description: Classifies generated images; blocks/flags unsafe output.

nodes.label: Notification / Stream
nodes.description: Pushes completion (or streams progress) to the client.

functional: Submit a text prompt (with options: size, count, model) and get generated image(s).
Screen prompts and outputs for disallowed content.
Show job status; deliver results when ready (poll or push).
Support tiers/priorities (free vs paid vs realtime).
nonFunctional: GPUs are scarce and expensive — maximize utilization, never idle them.
Generation takes seconds, so processing is async; the request returns a job id fast.
Safety is mandatory on input and output.
Fairness: a flood of free-tier requests must not starve paid/realtime users.
Resilient: a worker or job failure must not lose the request or strand a GPU.

capacity.label: GPUs
capacity.note: The bottleneck resource; utilization is the headline metric.

capacity.label: Result size
capacity.note: Stored in object storage, served via CDN.

api.description: Submit a generation job. Returns a job id immediately; processing is async.
Poll job status / get result URLs when ready.

patterns.name: Async job + status
patterns.what: Accept the request, return a job id, process on the GPU fleet asynchronously.
patterns.whenToUse: Expensive work (seconds+) that can't block the request.

patterns.name: Pre-inference safety gate
patterns.what: Screen the prompt before spending any GPU; screen the output before delivery.

patterns.name: Priority / fair scheduling
patterns.what: Per-tier queues + fair-share so paid/realtime aren't starved by free traffic.
patterns.whenToUse: Multi-tier demand on a scarce shared resource.

patterns.name: Inference batching
patterns.what: Group compatible jobs into one GPU batch to amortize model/overhead cost.
patterns.whenToUse: GPU inference where batching raises throughput dramatically.

patterns.name: Result store + CDN delivery
patterns.what: Store generated images in object storage and serve via CDN; notify on completion.
patterns.whenToUse: Large binary results read after async completion.

patterns.name: Idempotent retryable jobs
patterns.what: Jobs are keyed and re-runnable so a worker crash doesn't lose or duplicate work.
patterns.whenToUse: Long GPU jobs on a fleet that can fail mid-run.

steps.title: 1. Async Intake: Accept, Persist, Enqueue
description: Generation takes seconds, so it can't be synchronous. The API validates the request, checks the user's quota, persists a job record, and enqueues it — returning a job id immediately (202).
Persisting before enqueueing means a crash never loses an accepted job; the queue is rebuildable from queued jobs. The client polls or subscribes for completion.
This async-job shape is the backbone: it decouples request rate from the (much smaller) GPU throughput.
steps.decisionPrompt: Why return a job id instead of holding the request open until the image is ready?

concepts.definition: Accept + persist + enqueue, return an id, process later, deliver async.
concepts.whyItMatters: Keeps the request fast and absorbs bursts the GPU fleet can't serve instantly.
concepts.example: POST -> 202 {jobId}; client polls GET until status=done.

whyNow: The async-job decision shapes the whole system; it's what lets scarce GPUs be a queue-fed resource rather than a request-blocking one.

recap.after: Jobs durably accepted and queued; fast response.
recap.newRisk: Running disallowed prompts wastes scarce GPU and risks policy violations — screen first.

steps.title: 2. Safety Gates on Input and Output
description: Safety is mandatory and bookends the pipeline. Screen the prompt before any GPU work: a disallowed prompt is rejected at intake, saving expensive compute and preventing policy violations.
Screen the output too: classify the generated image and block/flag unsafe results before they're delivered, since a benign-looking prompt can still produce disallowed content.
Input screening is cheap and synchronous at intake; output screening runs right after inference, before the result is stored/served. Log decisions for audit.
steps.decisionPrompt: Why screen both the prompt AND the generated image, not just the prompt?

concepts.definition: Screen inputs before compute and outputs before delivery.
concepts.whyItMatters: Prompts and outputs fail independently; both gates are needed for policy compliance.
concepts.example: Reject a disallowed prompt; also block an unsafe image from an innocuous prompt.

whyNow: Safety is a hard requirement unique to generative systems; placing it before compute (cost) and before delivery (policy) is the key design point.

recap.after: Input + output safety gates protect cost and policy.
recap.newRisk: Demand far exceeds GPU capacity — jobs must be scheduled fairly across tiers.

traps.why: Models can produce disallowed content from innocuous prompts.

steps.title: 3. Priority and Fair Scheduling
description: GPUs are the scarce bottleneck and demand is bursty and multi-tier. Use per-tier priority queues (realtime > paid > free) so high-value/latency-sensitive work goes first, with fair-share so a flood of free requests doesn't starve paid users entirely.
A scheduler pulls from the queues honoring priority + fairness and assigns work to GPU workers. Quotas/rate limits at intake keep any one user from monopolizing the fleet.
When demand exceeds capacity, queue depth grows and you communicate wait/ETA — or shed/deprioritize free-tier — rather than dropping work.
steps.decisionPrompt: Free users send 100x the volume of paid users. How do you keep paid latency low without starving free entirely?

concepts.definition: Order work by tier priority while guaranteeing each tier some share.
concepts.whyItMatters: Protects high-value latency while avoiding total starvation of lower tiers.
concepts.example: Realtime jumps the queue; free gets a capped slice of GPU time.

whyNow: Scheduling scarce GPUs across tiers is the core resource-allocation problem; fairness is the requirement candidates most often miss.

recap.after: Tiered, fair scheduling of jobs to workers.
recap.newRisk: Even well-scheduled, per-job GPU overhead wastes the scarce resource — batch it.

steps.title: 4. GPU Inference with Batching
description: Running one prompt per GPU pass wastes the hardware: model load and per-call overhead dominate. The scheduler batches compatible jobs (same model/size) into one GPU pass, dramatically raising throughput per GPU.
Workers keep the model weights resident (loaded from the model store once) and process batches; trade a little latency (waiting to fill a batch) for large utilization gains — a tunable batch window.
Output goes through the safety filter, then to the result store. Workers report status back to the job store.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do you raise images-per-GPU-hour without buying more GPUs?

concepts.definition: Grouping compatible requests into one GPU forward pass.
concepts.whyItMatters: Amortizes fixed overhead, multiplying throughput on the scarce resource.
concepts.example: Wait up to 50ms to batch 8 same-size prompts into one pass.

whyNow: Batching is the specific GPU-economics lever; naming the latency-vs-utilization batch window is the staff-level insight.

recap.newRisk: Results are large binaries that many clients fetch — and jobs/workers can fail.

bottlenecks.issue: Cold model load stalls a worker.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Keep weights resident; pin workers to a model; warm pools per model.

steps.title: 5. Result Delivery
description: Generated images (MBs each) go to object storage; serve them via CDN so the read fanout doesn't hit the workers or origin. The job record holds the result keys/URLs.
Notify the client on completion: a push/stream for realtime UIs, or polling for simple clients. For interactive use, you can stream progress (intermediate diffusion previews).
Signed, expiring URLs protect results; keep them only as long as needed per the user's plan.
steps.decisionPrompt: Thousands of clients fetch their finished images. What keeps that load off the GPU workers?
whyNow: Delivery completes the async loop; CDN offload + completion notification is the standard, expected result path.

concepts.definition: Store the result, notify/poll for completion, serve via CDN.
concepts.whyItMatters: Decouples large-result reads from the expensive compute tier.
concepts.example: Worker stores image -> job done -> push URL -> client fetches from CDN.

recap.newRisk: Long jobs on a flaky GPU fleet — failures must not lose work or strand GPUs.

steps.title: 6. Reliability, Utilization, and Cost
description: Make jobs idempotent and retryable (keyed by job id + attempt): a worker crash returns the job to the queue and another worker re-runs it; the partial output is discarded. Dead-letter repeatedly-failing jobs.
Keep GPUs busy: autoscale workers from queue depth, keep warm model pools to avoid cold starts, and consider cheaper spot/preemptible GPUs for free-tier batch work (with re-queue on preemption).
Track utilization and queue wait as the core operational signals; the whole design exists to keep the scarce, expensive GPUs near-fully utilized while honoring fairness and safety.
steps.decisionPrompt: A GPU worker dies mid-generation. What happens to that job and that GPU?
whyNow: Closing on reliability + utilization + cost ties the design to the headline metric (don't idle/waste GPUs) and the resilience requirement.

recap.after: Crash-safe jobs; autoscaled, warm, cost-optimized GPU fleet.
recap.newRisk: Spot preemption and autoscale lag — bounded by re-queue, warm pools, and queue-depth scaling.

failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The job re-enters the queue and another worker re-runs it idempotently; the GPU is reclaimed.
failureDrills.mitigation: Idempotent jobs + at-least-once queue + health-driven reassignment.

failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Its in-flight job is re-queued (and re-prioritized if needed).
failureDrills.mitigation: Treat spot capacity as preemptible; re-queue on loss.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Text-to-Image Generation Platform
finalDesign.description: The API screens the prompt (input safety), enforces quota, persists a job, and enqueues it by tier — returning a job id fast. A fair-share priority scheduler pulls from per-tier queues and assigns batched, compatible jobs to GPU workers that keep model weights resident. Outputs pass the output-safety filter, land in object storage, and are served via CDN; the client is notified or polls. Jobs are idempotent and retryable so worker/spot failures re-queue without loss; the GPU fleet autoscales from queue depth with warm model pools to maximize utilization of the scarce, expensive hardware.

functional.how: Async job: enqueue, GPU inference, deliver via CDN.

nonFunctional.how: Job id returned immediately; processed off the request.
Input + output filters before compute and delivery.
Idempotent retryable jobs; re-queue on crash/preemption.

say: Confirm async generation on scarce, expensive GPUs.
Pin mandatory safety and multi-tier fairness.
Establish utilization as the headline metric.
Async intake: screen, persist, enqueue, return job id.
Deliver via object store + CDN, notify on completion.
Fair-share scheduling and inference batching for utilization.
Idempotent jobs, autoscaling, warm pools, spot capacity.
Tradeoffs: batch window (latency vs utilization), fairness vs priority, spot cost vs preemption.
Mention progress streaming and model versioning as extensions.

expectations: Async job to a GPU worker, deliver via storage.
May miss batching, fairness, and output safety.
Reasons about batch window and GPU utilization quantitatively.
Designs fair-share across tiers and spot/autoscale.
Discusses warm pools, model versioning, and cost precisely.

followUps: How do you tune the batch window to trade latency for utilization?
How would you stream progress / intermediate previews to the client?
How do you roll out a new model version without downtime?
How would you use spot/preemptible GPUs safely for free-tier work?
How do you stop one user from monopolizing the GPU fleet?
